FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 4524 children aged 8-11 years from 20 sites; first annual curated release; cross-sectional observational study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multivariable linear mixed-effects models examining relationship between movement behaviours (physical activity, screen time, sleep) and global cognition measured with NIH Toolbox

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper; The Lancet. Child & adolescent health, 2018

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure variable is 'recreational screen time' defined as composite spanning TV, console, phone with no per-modality breakdown isolating smartphone or SM. Criteria: 'Composite screen time totals that bundle TV/console/phone with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM' do NOT qualify. No separate smartphone/SM-only measure paired with individual-trait-level variable.

notes: Paper examines 24-hour movement guidelines and cognition but uses bundled screen time composite without phone/SM isolation.

FT_IC1.evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study, the Netherlands (n=2354). This is the Dutch ABCD cohort, not the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Criteria explicitly exclude: 'Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD)' as NOT_MET.

notes: Paper is from Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD), not the U.S. ABCD Study. Fails at FT-IC1; cohort is non-U.S.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline mean age 9.9 years; N=11,878 children with baseline data, analytic sample N=9,475; annual follow-up assessments from 2016 onward; ABCD 5.0 data release referenced

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and first smartphone ownership age, with odds ratios (ORs) and 95% CIs reported

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper; Pediatrics, Volume 157, Issue 2, February 2026; e2025073891

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcome is smartphone ownership age (binary: <10 years vs. >=10 years); paired only with sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental marital status, parental education). Criteria explicitly exclude: 'Other side is demographic-only (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, household composition) — paper-level NOT_MET if no individual-trait-level analysis exists anywhere.' No analysis pairs smartphone variable with individual-trait-level outcomes (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, etc.).

notes: Paper examines sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership but lacks individual-trait-level outcome variables. This is epidemiology of device ownership timing, not SM/phone-trait association.
